title: Articulation in Motion: Prior-free Part Mobility Analysis for Articulated Objects By Dynamic-Static Disentanglement
name: aim-prior-free-articulation
short_hypothesis: By analyzing an object interaction video instead of two static states, a proposed dual-Gaussian representation can disentangle static and dynamic components. This clean separation of motion cues will enable a subsequent sequential RANSAC algorithm to perform robust, prior-free part segmentation and articulation analysis, automatically determining the number of parts and their kinematics.
related_work: Recent methods for articulated object analysis typically rely on observing the object in two distinct states (start and end). These approaches perform per-point segmentation and optimize articulation, but often require a priori knowledge of the number of parts. Their performance degrades when there is no clear geometric correspondence between the two states, such as when new parts are revealed during motion (e.g., the interior of a refrigerator). This reliance on two static states and prior knowledge about the part count limits their application and robustness.
abstract: Articulated objects are ubiquitous in daily life. Our goal is to achieve a high-quality reconstruction, segmentation of independent moving parts, and analysis of articulation. Recent methods analyse two different articulation states and perform per-point part segmentation, optimising per-part articulation using cross-state correspondences, given a priori knowledge of the number of parts. Such assumptions greatly limit their applications and performance. Their robustness is reduced when objects cannot be clearly visible in both states. To address these issues, in this paper, we present a new framework, Articulation in Motion (AiM). We infer part-level decomposition, articulation kinematics, and reconstruct an interactive 3D digital replica from a user–object interaction video and a start-state scan. We propose a dual-Gaussian scene representation that is learned from an initial 3DGS scan of the object and a video that shows the movement of separate parts. It uses motion cues to segment the object into parts and assign articulation joints. Subsequently, a robust, sequential RANSAC is employed to achieve part mobility analysis without any part-level structural priors, which clusters moving primitives into rigid parts and estimates kinematics while automatically determining the number of parts. The proposed approach separates the object into parts, each represented as a 3D Gaussian set, enabling high-quality rendering.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of the proposed method (AIM) on part segmentation, reconstruction quality, and articulation estimation accuracy against state-of-the-art two-state methods.
experiments.Method: The experiment will use a synthetic dataset rendered from PartNet-Mobility objects, including challenging two-part, three-part, and multi-part objects where interior parts are gradually revealed. AIM will be given a multi-view scan of the start state and an interaction video (200-500 frames). Baselines will be given 100 random views of the start and end states. The method will be compared against baselines: PARIS†, DTA, and ArtGS. A baseline version of the proposed method, Ours-b, which uses a single deformable 3DGS, will also be evaluated.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Part segmentation performance using 3D Intersection-over-Union (IoU).', 'Reconstruction quality using bi-directional Chamfer Distance (mm).', 'Articulation estimation accuracy using Axis Angle Error (°), Axis Position Error (0.1m), and Part Motion (° or m).']

experiments.Description: To analyze the effectiveness of individual components of the AIM framework through a series of ablation studies.
experiments.Method: The experiment will be conducted on complex objects from the synthetic dataset (Storage-47648, Table-31249). The full model will be compared against several ablated versions: one without the start state scan, one without the static-during-motion detection (SDMD) module, one without the dual-Gaussian representation (using a single deformable 3DGS), and one without sequential RANSAC (attempting DB-SCAN or K-means instead).
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Average Axis Angle Error (Ang_avg)', 'Average Axis Pose Error (Pose_avg)', 'Average Part Motion Error (Motion_ang/avg)', 'Average Chamfer Distance on moving parts (CD-m_avg)', 'Average 3D IoU on dynamic parts (3D IoU_d_avg)']

experiments.Description: To evaluate the method's performance on real-world data captured from a first-person perspective.
experiments.Method: Data will be captured using Meta Project Aria Glasses, recording a user interacting with an oven and a storage cabinet. The video will be processed using COLMAP to estimate camera poses. The performance of AIM will be qualitatively assessed on these sequences.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Qualitative comparison of rendered views against ground truth.', 'Visual inspection of rendered part masks from the dual-Gaussian representation.', 'Qualitative assessment of the estimated articulation parameters.']

experiments.Description: To compare the proposed method against a pre-trained segmentation-driven approach to demonstrate its effectiveness beyond the self-contained setting.
experiments.Method: The evaluation will use an overlapping subset of objects from the official Video2Articulation dataset, including four two-part objects and one three-part object. The proposed method will be compared against the Video2Articulation baseline.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Axis Angle Error (°)', 'Axis Position Error (0.1m)', 'Chamfer Distance on moving parts (CD-m)', 'Chamfer Distance on static parts (CD-s)']

risk_factors: We do not require the use of depth sensing, as done by most prior art techniques; however, we do utilise a video that captures the DoFs of the object's parts.
Capturing the whole geometry of internal parts, such as drawers, an extended blade of a knife, or the blades of a pair of scissors, requires the full disassembly of the articulated object.
The generated geometry is limited to the visible geometry and, as such, may be limited in its application for developing interactive models.

From the real-world data, we observe that when motion introduces structural ambiguities—particularly those caused by specular reflections, such as the glass door of the oven in the video—our vanilla 3DGS-based reconstruction in AIM can be affected.

title: Knowledge Fusion of Large Language Models
name: LLM-Knowledge-Fusion-FuseLLM
short_hypothesis: It is possible to combine the capabilities of multiple, existing, architecturally-diverse Large Language Models (LLMs) into a single, more potent target LLM. This can be achieved by externalizing the collective knowledge and unique strengths of the source LLMs via their generative probability distributions, and then transferring this fused knowledge to the target LLM through lightweight continual training.
related_work: Prior work on integrating capabilities from multiple models includes ensemble methods and weight merging. Ensemble methods are impractical for LLMs due to high memory and inference time requirements, and they do not facilitate fine-tuning into a single model. Weight merging requires the models to have uniform network architectures, which is a significant limitation. Knowledge distillation is also related, but typically aims for model compression where the student model's performance lags behind the teacher. This work proposes a method to fuse knowledge from architecturally diverse LLMs into a single model that is anticipated to surpass the performance of any individual source model.
abstract: While training large language models (LLMs) from scratch can generate models with distinct functionalities and strengths, it comes at significant costs and may result in redundant capabilities. Alternatively, a cost-effective and compelling approach is to merge existing pre-trained LLMs into a more potent model. However, due to the varying architectures of these LLMs, directly blending their weights is impractical. In this paper, we introduce the notion of knowledge fusion for LLMs, aimed at combining the capabilities of existing LLMs and transferring them into a single LLM. By leveraging the generative distributions of source LLMs, we externalize their collective knowledge and unique strengths, thereby potentially elevating the capabilities of the target model beyond those of any individual source LLM.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the general reasoning ability of the fused LLM compared to its source models.
experiments.Method: The experiment will be conducted on the Big-Bench Hard (BBH) benchmark, which contains 23 multiple-choice and 4 free-form generation tasks. Predictions will be generated using few-shot chain-of-thought (CoT) prompts. The source models for fusion are Llama-2 7B, OpenLLaMA 7B, and MPT 7B. The target model is another Llama-2 7B. Baselines for comparison will be the original source LLMs and a Llama-2 7B model continually trained on the MiniPile dataset using only the standard causal language modeling objective (Llama-2 CLM).

experiments.Description: To evaluate the commonsense capability of the fused LLM.
experiments.Method: The experiment will use the Common Sense (CS) benchmark, comprising 5 multiple-choice tasks: ARC easy and challenge, BoolQ, HellaSwag, and OpenBookQA. A likelihood-based zero-shot evaluation will be conducted. The source, target, and baseline models are the same as in the reasoning experiment (Llama-2 7B, OpenLLaMA 7B, MPT 7B as sources; Llama-2 7B as target; original LLMs and Llama-2 CLM as baselines).

experiments.Description: To assess the coding ability of the fused LLM across multiple programming languages.
experiments.Method: The experiment will be performed on the MultiPL-E benchmark, specifically using zero-shot code generation for 10 programming languages in the HumanEval category. The source, target, and baseline models are the same as in the previous experiments.

experiments.Description: To compare the performance of FUSELLM against traditional knowledge distillation.
experiments.Method: FUSELLM will be used to integrate three 7B models (Llama-2, OpenLLaMA, MPT). This will be compared against a conventional knowledge distillation setup where a Llama-2 7B model is trained to distill knowledge from a larger Llama-2 13B model. The evaluation is planned across the BBH, CS, and ME benchmarks.

experiments.Description: To compare the efficacy of FUSELLM with model ensemble and weight merging methods on architecturally identical models.
experiments.Method: A scenario will be simulated where three distinct LLMs with identical structures are created by continually training a Pythia 1B model on three different domain-specific corpora (PhilPapers, NIH ExPorter, USPTO Backgrounds). These three models will then be fused using FUSELLM, an ensemble method (weighted average of probabilities), and a weight merging method.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the applicability of FUSELLM for fusing instruction-tuned models.
experiments.Method: Source models will be created by fine-tuning Llama-2, OpenLLaMA, and MPT on instruction datasets (Evol-Instruct, ShareGPT, Open-Platypus). FUSELLM will then be used to fuse their knowledge into a target model (the instruction-tuned Llama-2).
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Instruction-following performance on the Vicuna Benchmark, judged by GPT-4.']

risk_factors: The thoughtful selection of the training corpus can be a crucial consideration, particularly with regard to its relevance to downstream tasks.
In scenarios where the capabilities of source LLMs vary significantly, the fusion function appears to be crucial in effectively combining their respective strengths.

FUSELLM occasionally exhibits degraded performance on tasks such as Geometric Shapes and Word Sorting, which could be attributed to two reasons. First, the other source LLMs, apart from Llama-2, perform poorly on these tasks, affecting the fusion results. Second, the relevance between the continual training dataset and downstream tasks also contributes to the performance degradation.
The distortions introduced by the straightforward weighted summation used in AvgCE, which may diminish the distinct advantages of individual LLMs.

title: Latent Diffusion with LLMs for Reasoning
name: latent-diffusion-for-reasoning
short_hypothesis: Combining latent diffusion models (LDMs) with an encoder-decoder transformer architecture provides a scalable way to address the fundamental shortcomings of autoregressive models for complex reasoning. This is achieved by performing iterative reasoning in a semantically rich latent space, which is not handicapped by the sequential order of tokens and allows for non-sequential, self-correcting reasoning steps.
related_work: Autoregressive large language models (LLMs) are the de-facto standard for natural language generation but struggle with complex reasoning. Their limitations stem from their autoregressive nature, where compute is constant per token and generation is strictly sequential, preventing them from solving easier subproblems first. Prior approaches like Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompting, longer context, and recurrent models have attempted to address these issues but are not general solutions. Discrete text diffusion models (e.g., Diffusion-of-Thought) have shown promise for self-correction but lack the training efficiency of traditional LLMs. This work proposes to merge the benefits of both worlds by using a latent diffusion model on top of representations from a pretrained encoder-decoder LLM.
abstract: Despite the widespread adoption of large language models with hundreds of billions of parameters, these models still struggle on complex reasoning benchmarks. In this paper, we argue that the autoregressive nature of current language models are not suited for reasoning due to fundamental limitations, and that reasoning requires slow accumulation of knowledge through time. We show that combining latent diffusion models with an encoder-decoder transformer architecture provides a scalable way to address some of the fundamental shortcomings posed by autoregressive models. Diffusion models can arrive at predictions through many forward passes in latent space, and their reasoning is not handicapped by the order of the tokens in the dataset. We propose that latent diffusion language models is a feasible approach towards scalable language models that have general complex reasoning abilities.

experiments.Description: To analyze the performance of the proposed hybrid architecture on downstream arithmetic reasoning tasks.
experiments.Method: The proposed Latent Diffusion model is to be evaluated on custom-created problem sets for single digit addition (3-5 numbers), double digit addition, and single digit three-operation tasks. For single digit addition, Chain-of-Thought reasoning chains are provided as the target. The performance will be compared against fine-tuned BART and various sizes of GPT-3 (400M, 13B, 175B).

experiments.Description: To study the benefits of the proposed latent diffusion augmented LLM on a mock spatial reasoning problem that requires non-trivial reasoning chains to compute the first token.
experiments.Method: A custom spatial reasoning task is created where the model must perform a series of rotations based on input numbers. The proposed Latent Diffusion model will be tested on this task and its performance will be compared against a fine-tuned BART baseline.

experiments.Description: To ablate different architectural variants, diffusion sequence lengths, and model layers to determine the best architecture for scaling and to study the effect of different input text conditioning methods.
experiments.Method: Ablation studies are planned on the Common Crawl (C4) dataset. These include: 1) Comparing different input text conditioning architectures (In-Context, Cross-Attention, AdaLN-Zero). 2) Varying the number of model layers (6, 12, 24). 3) Varying the diffusion sequence length (16, 32, 64). 4) Comparing the use of different pretrained encoder-decoder backbones (BART-base vs. BART-large).

risk_factors: A notable limitation of diffusion is that it is relatively inefficient to train compared to conventional language models.
One hypothesis is that there is a combinatorial explosion as more tokens are diffused at once, hence the gradients are not as well-behaved (noisy gradient landscapes).
However, due to its inefficiencies and other potential obstacles, it remains uncertain how far we can practically scale such architectures with current hardware and algorithms.

We've found that generation length decreases with longer training times from preliminary experiments which led to uncomparable BertScores since longer sequences have higher BertScores on average and that shorter sequences have lower Perplexity on average.
Since experiments are trained with different hyperparameters for different learned generation lengths, metrics cannot be compared between different experiments.

title: Building Neural Networks that are Robust to Adversarial Examples Using Probabilistic Loss Function
name: probabilistic-loss-for-adversarial-robustness
short_hypothesis: Putting a non-zero probability mass on all classes, with the correct class carrying the highest weight, may reduce over-fitting and render a trained DNN less vulnerable to adversarial attacks by modifying the standard cross-entropy loss function.
related_work: Prior defenses against adversarial examples include adversarial training (computationally expensive), defensive distillation and ensemble methods (overhead of multiple models), and input transformations. This work proposes a method that modifies the loss function directly, aiming to avoid the overhead of training additional models or augmenting the input data.
abstract: Adversarial examples are an Achilles heel of deep neural networks, robbing them of their functional performance in mission-critical applications. This work proposes a novel method of making deep neural networks robust to adversarial examples by modifying the loss function to a soft version of cross-entropy loss for classification problems. For regression problems, the data distribution is examined using Bayesian techniques (Gaussian Mixture Model) and the loss function is modified using a posterior probability distribution. The approach is justified using mathematical derivation.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the effectiveness of the proposed probabilistic loss function in defending against adversarial attacks on the MNIST image classification task.
experiments.Method: A deep neural network based on the Inception CNN architecture will be trained on the MNIST dataset. One model will be trained using the standard cross-entropy loss function as a baseline. A second model will be trained using the proposed calibrated probabilistic loss function (calibrated via Algorithm 1). Both trained models will be subjected to FGSM and Carlini-Wagner L2 attacks.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Classification accuracy on adversarial examples generated by FGSM and Carlini-Wagner L2 attacks.']

experiments.Description: To evaluate the defense's effectiveness on a more complex, large-scale image classification task using the Imagenet dataset.
experiments.Method: A model will be trained on the Imagenet dataset using the proposed probabilistic loss function. Its robustness will be tested against the Carlini-Wagner attack.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Classification accuracy on adversarial examples generated by the Carlini-Wagner attack.']

risk_factors: For regression problems, we do not know the number of clusters. Therefore, this becomes a hyper-parameter.
Investigating the effectiveness of other non-Gaussian priors is an avenue for future research.

title: Counterintuitive RL: The Hidden Value of Acting Bad
name: maxmin-td-learning
short_hypothesis: A theoretically-founded improvement to temporal difference learning can be achieved by taking actions that minimize the state-action value function, especially early in training. This counterintuitive strategy is proposed to increase the information gain from environment interactions by generating experiences with higher temporal difference errors, thereby accelerating learning at zero additional computational cost.
related_work: Experience collection in deep reinforcement learning is an open problem. While provably optimal techniques exist for tabular or bandit settings (e.g., count-based methods), they do not transfer well to high-dimensional state spaces and often introduce significant computational complexity. Consequently, practitioners still rely on simple, naive techniques like e-greedy. This paper aims to bridge the gap by introducing a theoretically motivated, computationally efficient, and simple experience collection strategy for deep RL.
abstract: Learning to make sequential decisions solely from interacting with an environment without any supervision has been achieved by the initial installation of deep neural networks as function approximators to represent and learn a value function in high-dimensional MDPs. Reinforcement learning policies face exponentially growing state spaces in experience collection in high dimensional MDPs resulting in a dichotomy between computational complexity and policy success. In our paper we focus on the agent’s interaction with the environment in a high-dimensional MDP during the learning phase and we introduce a theoretically-founded novel method based on experiences obtained through extremum actions. Our analysis and method provides a theoretical basis for effective, accelerated and efficient experience collection, and further comes with zero additional computational cost while leading to significant acceleration of training in deep reinforcement learning.

experiments.Description: To provide intuition for the proposed method in a simple, controlled environment, an experiment is planned on a toy problem called the chain MDP.
experiments.Method: The experiment will be conducted on a chain MDP with n=10 states. The proposed MaxMin TD Learning method will be compared with the e-greedy action selection method and the upper confidence bound (UCB) method within a tabular Q-learning framework.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Learning curves showing Average Cumulative Return vs. Environment Interactions.']

experiments.Description: To evaluate the sample efficiency of the proposed method in a low-data regime across a wide range of tasks.
experiments.Method: Experiments will be conducted on the Arcade Learning Environment (ALE) 100K benchmark. The proposed MaxMin TD Learning exploration strategy will be integrated into DDQN and QRDQN algorithms. It will be compared against two baselines: the standard e-greedy exploration strategy and NoisyNetworks.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Human normalized median scores', 'Human normalized 20th percentile scores', 'Human normalized 80th percentile scores']

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of the proposed method in a standard, large-data regime with extended training.
experiments.Method: Experiments will be conducted on the Arcade Learning Environment (ALE) with 200 million frames of training (50 million environment interactions). The proposed MaxMin TD Learning algorithm will be compared against the original version of the DDQN algorithm with e-greedy training on a selection of games (e.g., StarGunner, Bowling, Surround, BankHeist, JamesBond, Amidar, Gravitar, Tennis).
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Learning curves showing game scores vs. environment interactions.']

experiments.Description: To empirically investigate the theoretical claim that taking the minimum Q-value action results in transitions with higher temporal difference (TD).
experiments.Method: During training on the ALE 100K benchmark and the chain MDP, the temporal difference will be recorded for batches of transitions. The TD obtained by the MaxMin TD Learning method will be compared to the TD obtained by e-greedy-based learning and other baselines like NoisyNetworks.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Temporal difference values', 'Normalized TD Gain, defined as 1 + (TD_method - TD_e-greedy) / |TD_e-greedy|']

title: Offline Congestion Games: How Feedback Type Affects Data Coverage Requirement
name: offline-congestion-games-feedback-types
short_hypothesis: For offline congestion games, where the action space can be exponentially large, it is possible to efficiently recover an approximate Nash Equilibrium (NE) by designing novel data coverage assumptions tailored to different types of feedback (facility-level, agent-level, and game-level), thereby avoiding the prohibitive sample complexity incurred by existing assumptions for general-sum games.
related_work: Existing theoretical work on offline multi-agent games proposes a unilateral coverage assumption for learning a Nash Equilibrium (NE). However, when applied to congestion games, this assumption results in a sample complexity that scales with the number of actions, which can be exponentially large and thus impractical. While online learning in congestion games has been studied, the offline setting, where an algorithm learns from a fixed, pre-collected dataset, is less understood, especially regarding how different types of reward feedback (e.g., per-facility, per-agent, or per-game) affect the data requirements for efficient learning.
abstract: This paper investigates when one can efficiently recover an approximate Nash Equilibrium (NE) in offline congestion games. The existing dataset coverage assumption in offline general-sum games inevitably incurs a dependency on the number of actions, which can be exponentially large in congestion games. We consider three different types of feedback with decreasing revealed information. Starting from the facility-level (a.k.a., semi-bandit) feedback, we propose a novel one-unit deviation coverage condition and show a pessimism-type algorithm that can recover an approximate NE. For the agent-level (a.k.a., bandit) feedback setting, we show the one-unit deviation coverage condition is not sufficient. On the other hand, we convert the game to multi-agent linear bandits and propose that with a generalized data coverage assumption in offline linear bandits, we can efficiently recover the approximate NE. Lastly, we consider a novel type of feedback, the game-level feedback where only the total reward from all agents is revealed. We propose that with a stronger version of the data coverage assumption for linear bandits, we can recover an approximate NE.

experiments.Description: To empirically verify the theoretical results for the facility-level feedback setting by investigating: (i) if the proposed algorithm can recover the Nash Equilibrium (NE) from datasets satisfying the proposed coverage assumption, (ii) how fast the algorithm converges as sample size increases, and (iii) how the convergence rate varies with the number of players.
experiments.Method: The experiment uses a modified version of the Braess paradox, a canonical congestion game with 5 facilities (roads) and a variable number of players (m). The proposed Surrogate Minimization algorithm (Algorithm 1) is evaluated. Offline datasets are generated using two different exploration policies: 1) a `random` policy where each player selects actions uniformly, and 2) a `facility` policy designed to satisfy the proposed `Assumption 2` (one-unit deviation coverage) by covering all facility configurations achievable from unilateral deviations from the NE.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Performance gap of the output policy, which measures its distance from being an NE. This will be plotted against the dataset sample size (n) for different numbers of players (m).']

title: Ferret-UI Lite: Lessons from Building Small On-Device GUI Agents
name: ferret-ui-lite
short_hypothesis: A compact, end-to-end 3B multimodal LLM can be built to be a competitive GUI agent for on-device deployment by curating a diverse mixture of real and synthetic GUI data, strengthening inference-time performance with chain-of-thought reasoning and visual tool-use, and employing a two-stage training strategy of supervised fine-tuning (SFT) followed by reinforcement learning (RL) with verifiable rewards.
related_work: The majority of existing GUI agents are built on large foundation models, which are computationally expensive and have high latency, making them unsuitable for on-device deployment where low latency, privacy, and connectivity are critical. While end-to-end agents that directly map screenshots to actions are an attractive alternative, building competitive small-scale versions remains a challenge. This work explores strategies to adapt modern GUI agent techniques (SFT, synthetic data generation, CoT, RL) to the resource-constrained setting of small, on-device models.
abstract: Developing autonomous agents that effectively interact with Graphic User Interfaces (GUIs) is a challenging open problem, especially for small on-device models. This paper proposes FERRET-UI LITE, a compact, end-to-end GUI agent designed to operate across mobile, web, and desktop platforms. The proposed method involves building a 3B agent by curating a diverse GUI data mixture from real and synthetic sources, strengthening inference-time performance through chain-of-thought reasoning and visual tool-use, and applying reinforcement learning with designed rewards.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the model's GUI grounding performance, which is the ability to map natural language instructions to target elements on a screen.
experiments.Method: The 3B FERRET-UI LITE model will be evaluated on the ScreenSpot-V2, ScreenSpot-Pro, and OSWorld-G benchmarks. Its performance will be compared against other small-scale models like UI-R1 (3B), UITars (2B), QwenVL 2.5 (3B), and Jedi (3B), as well as larger models such as UITars (7B), GTA1 (7B), and GUI-OWL (7B).

experiments.Description: To conduct an offline evaluation of the model's agentic planning and grounding capabilities in a mobile environment.
experiments.Method: The model will be evaluated on the Android Control dataset, which features two task types: low-level tasks (single-step instructions) and high-level tasks (multi-step global goals). Performance will be compared against baselines including InternVL-2 (4B), OSAtlas (4B), and Qwen2-VL (7B).

experiments.Description: To conduct an online evaluation of the model's GUI navigation capabilities in fully functional, dynamic environments.
experiments.Method: The model will be evaluated on two benchmarks: (1) AndroidWorld, a dynamic Android environment, with performance compared to models like QwenVL 2.5 (3B), ScaleCUA (3B), and UITars 1.5 (7B). (2) OSWorld-Verified, a benchmark for computer use simulation, with performance compared against models like ScaleCUA (3B), Kimi-VL (3B), OpenCUA (A3B), and Claude-4-Sonnet.

experiments.Description: To investigate the capabilities and limitations of small GUI agents through a series of ablation studies.
experiments.Method: Ablation experiments will be conducted to study: (1) The effect of different data mixture ratios of navigation and grounding data. (2) The impact of incorporating synthetic high-resolution data. (3) The effectiveness of synthetic Chain-of-Thought (CoT) data. (4) The contribution of SFT vs. RL training variants, including a zoom-in operation during RL. (5) The impact of different RL reward designs (action type, sparse grounding, dense grounding).

risk_factors: its performance on multi-step navigation remains limited, underscoring the inherent challenges of developing lightweight, on-device agents capable of robust long-horizon reasoning.
While small models can benefit from reinforcement learning, they are sensitive to RL reward designs, underscoring the difficulty of designing robust rewards across heterogeneous UI agentic tasks.
CoT yields structured reasoning, but only in the context of strong grounding and supervision.
Careful RL reward design is critical. Small models are highly sensitive to reward formulation.

title: Local Control Networks (LCNs): Optimizing Flexibility in Neural Network Data Pattern Capture
name: local-control-networks
short_hypothesis: Employing a uniform, fixed activation function across all nodes in a neural network is suboptimal. Using distinct, learnable activation functions at each node, specifically by leveraging B-spline functions, will increase the network's flexibility, adaptability, and representational capacity, enabling it to model complex data patterns more effectively.
related_work: Traditional Multi-layer Perceptrons (MLPs) use a fixed activation function (e.g., ReLU, Sigmoid, Tanh) for all neurons, which can limit flexibility and lead to issues like 'dead neurons' or vanishing gradients. More recent architectures like Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (KANs) have introduced learnable activation functions on the edges of the network, offering increased flexibility but facing limitations in application and complexity. The proposed Local Control Networks (LCNs) aim to bridge this gap by enabling each neuron to have its own unique, learnable B-spline activation function within a standard network structure, offering a higher degree of flexibility and local adaptability without the architectural complexity of KANs.
abstract: The widespread use of multi-layer perceptrons (MLPs) often relies on a fixed activation function (e.g., ReLU, Sigmoid, Tanh) for all nodes within the hidden layers. While effective in many scenarios, this uniformity may limit the network's ability to capture complex data patterns. We argue that employing the same activation function at every node is suboptimal and propose leveraging different activation functions at each node to increase flexibility and adaptability. To achieve this, we introduce Local Control Networks (LCNs), which leverage B-spline functions to enable distinct activation curves at each node.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of the proposed LCN model on a variety of basic machine learning tasks and compare it against standard baselines.
experiments.Method: The experiment will be conducted on the Bank Marketing, Bean Classification, Spam Detection, and Telescope datasets. The proposed LCN model will be compared against conventional Multi-Layer Perceptrons (MLPs) with fixed activations and Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (KANs). To ensure a fair comparison, the total number of parameters will be standardized across all models, and LCN and KAN models will undergo hyperparameter tuning via grid search.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance and scalability of LCNs on computer vision tasks involving high-dimensional input spaces.
experiments.Method: The experiment will use the MNIST and Fashion-MNIST (FMNIST) datasets for image classification. The LCN model's performance will be compared to that of MLPs and KANs. Model configurations will be standardized by adjusting layers and neurons to have a comparable number of total parameters, with hyperparameters for LCN and KAN being tuned via grid search.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of LCNs on symbolic regression tasks, which involve approximating mathematical functions.
experiments.Method: The experiment will be performed on synthetic datasets designed for symbolic regression. The LCN model will be benchmarked against MLPs and KANs. The total number of parameters will be standardized across models for a fair comparison.

risk_factors: The marginal improvements of LCNs over MLPs and KANs reflect the simplicity of the datasets used, which follow the standards in (Yu et al., 2024).
On these basic tasks, MLPs already achieve strong performance, leaving limited room for improvement for LCNs and KANs.
However, the flexibility and localized activations of LCNs are expected to show greater advantages on more complex datasets with higher-dimensional inputs or intricate patterns.
